topic: migraine episode — venting about bad week

turns.content: I am having the worst week. I cannot think straight, everything is too loud and too bright and I have been useless for three days. I just need to complain to someone who will not try to fix me
I'm here, and I'm listening. No fixing, no advice unless you ask for it. What's going on?

migraine. a bad one, the kind that sits behind my left eye like a nail being slowly hammered in. it started Tuesday and it has not fully gone. it eases for a few hours and then comes crashing back. I missed two site visits, I had to cancel a meeting I had been preparing for all month, and I have been lying in a dark room with a cold cloth on my face like some Victorian heroine and I am furious about it

Three days with a migraine that keeps cycling back is brutal. And the frustration of watching your plans fall apart while you're physically pinned down — that's its own kind of suffering on top of the pain. Being angry about it makes complete sense.

the anger is almost worse than the pain, honestly. because the pain I am used to — I have lived with these migraines for over twenty years, they are an old enemy, I know their rhythms. but the disruption, the things I cannot do, the people I have to call and say 'I am sorry, I cannot come today' — that makes me feel like I am unreliable, like my body is making promises my head cannot keep

"Promises my head cannot keep" — that's a painfully accurate way to describe it. Twenty years of this and you're still showing up, still building a career, still making plans. That's not unreliable. That's someone who operates at full capacity despite a condition that periodically takes her offline. The people around you know the difference between someone who cancels because they don't care and someone who cancels because they're in genuine pain.

rationally I know that. but when I am lying there in the dark, unable to even look at my phone, the irrational voice takes over. it says: you are falling behind, people are losing patience, the work is piling up and you are doing nothing. and I know that voice is the migraine talking — pain distorts everything, it is like looking at the world through cracked glass — but in the moment it feels true

Pain absolutely distorts thinking — that's neurologically real, not just a metaphor. Chronic pain activates the same brain regions as anxiety and depression. So that voice telling you you're falling behind and people are losing patience isn't your rational assessment, it's your nervous system in crisis mode generating worst-case narratives.

The fact that you can identify it — "I know that voice is the migraine talking" — means you have a kind of clarity about your own mind that a lot of people never develop. Even if you can't silence the voice in the moment, knowing it's not truth is a form of resistance.

resistance. yes. Darwish wrote about resistance as a daily practice, not a grand gesture. I suppose lying in the dark and refusing to believe the voice that says I am failing — that is its own small resistance. although it does not feel heroic. it feels like being trapped in a room where someone is playing a single terrible note on a violin forever

A single terrible note on a violin forever — that's one of the most precise descriptions of a migraine I've ever heard. And no, it doesn't feel heroic. Resistance rarely does from the inside. From the inside it just feels like enduring.

But you're through three days of it and you're here, coherent, articulate, furious — which honestly is a better sign than resignation. The fury means your engine is still running. The migraine hasn't beaten you into passivity.

you are right about the fury. when I stop being angry about it is when I worry. the anger means I still have somewhere I want to be, something I want to do. today it has eased enough that I can look at a screen for a few minutes without wanting to die, so that is progress. tomorrow I might be able to work again. yalla, one hour at a time

One hour at a time is exactly right. And the fact that you can tolerate a screen right now — even for a few minutes — means the cycle is likely breaking. Don't rush back to full capacity. Let yourself come back slowly.

The site visits and the meeting can be rescheduled. The buildings will still be standing when you get there. Give yourself at least one more day of rest even if you feel better tomorrow.

I know, I know. I am terrible at resting. I am the kind of person who lies in bed and mentally drafts floor plans on the ceiling. but you are right — the buildings will wait. they have been waiting a hundred years, they can wait one more week for me. thank you for letting me vent. I apologize for being a storm cloud today

You don't need to apologize. Everyone needs a place to be a storm cloud sometimes, and after three days of a migraine you've more than earned it. I hope you wake up tomorrow and the note has finally stopped playing. Take care of yourself, Leila.
